  "content": {    "en": "## What This Guide Covers
This booklet prepares you for the Canadian citizenship process and test. It summarizes what the **Citizenship Regulations** require, especially **knowledge of Canada**, understanding the **rights and responsibilities of citizenship**, and **adequate knowledge of English or French**. It also points to key references, including **page 64** for how the knowledge requirement is assessed and **page 111** for contact numbers.

## How to Study Effectively
Start by **studying the guide carefully** and practising answers with a **friend or family member**. You can also contact a **local school**, **school board**, **college**, **community centre**, or immigrant-serving organization to find citizenship classes. The **Government of Canada** provides **free English or French language classes** to help applicants prepare.

## Application and Test Basics
When you apply, officials verify your **status**, confirm that you are **not prohibited from applying**, and check all eligibility requirements. Processing may take **several months**, so keep your current address updated with the **Call Centre** while your file is active. The citizenship assessment is usually a **written test**, but in some cases it can be an **interview**, and all test questions come from subject areas listed in the **Citizenship Regulations** and covered in this guide.

## Age Rule and What Happens Next
Applicants aged **55 and older** do **not** need to write the citizenship test. If you pass and meet the other requirements, you receive a **Notice to Appear to Take the Oath of Citizenship** that gives the ceremony **date, time, and place**. At the ceremony, you **take the Oath of Citizenship**, **sign the oath form**, and receive your **Canadian Citizenship Certificate**; if you do not pass, you receive a notice explaining next steps

t pass, you receive a notice explaining next stepsYou are encouraged to celebrate with **family and friends**.

material appears across **pages 8, 9, and 10**.",    "fr": "## Ce que couvre ce guide
Ce livret vous prépare au processus et à l'examen de citoyenneté canadienne. Il résume les exigences du **Règlement sur la citoyenneté**, surtout la **connaissance du Canada**, la compréhension des **droits et responsabilités de la citoyenneté** et une **connaissance suffisante de l'anglais ou du français**. Il renvoie aussi à des repères utiles, notamment la **page 64** (évaluation des connaissances) et la **page 111** (numéros de téléphone).

## Comment bien étudier
Commencez par **étudier ce guide attentivement** et entraînez-vous avec un **ami ou un membre de la famille**. Vous pouvez aussi communiquer avec une **école locale**, un **conseil scolaire**, un **collège**, un **centre communautaire** ou un organisme de services aux immigrants pour trouver des cours de citoyenneté. Le **gouvernement du Canada** offre gratuitement des **cours d'anglais ou de français** pour aider les demandeurs.

## Demande et examen : l'essentiel
Au moment de la demande, les autorités vérifient votre **statut**, confirment que vous n'êtes **pas interdit de présenter une demande**, puis valident tous les critères d'admissibilité. Le traitement peut prendre **plusieurs mois**; gardez donc votre adresse à jour auprès du **Centre d'appels** pendant l'étude du dossier. L'évaluation de citoyenneté est habituellement un **examen écrit**, mais elle peut aussi se faire en **entrevue**; toutes les questions proviennent des thèmes définis par le **Règlement sur la citoyenneté** et traités dans ce guide.

## Règle d'âge et étapes après l'examen
Les demandeurs de **55 ans et plus** n'ont **pas** à passer l'examen de citoyenneté. Si vous réussissez et remplissez les autres conditions, vous recevrez un **Avis de convocation pour prêter le serment de citoyenneté** indiquant la **date, l'heure et le lieu** de la cérémonie

nt la **date, l'heure et le lieu** de la cérémoniePendant la cérémonie, vous **prêtez le serment de citoyenneté**, **signez le formulaire de serment** et recevez votre **certificat de citoyenneté canadienne**; en cas d'échec, un avis explique les prochaines étapes. Vous êtes encouragé à célébrer avec votre **famille et vos amis**.

## Note de source
Dans l'édition imprimée, ce contenu d'introduction se trouve aux **pages 8, 9 et 10**."

me": "Rights and Responsibilities",
  "content": {    "en": "## Legal Foundations of Citizenship
Canadian citizenship combines rights and duties shaped by history, law, and shared values. Canadian law draws from **Parliament**, **provincial legislatures**, **English common law**, the **civil code of France**, and an unwritten constitutional tradition inherited from **Great Britain**. Together, these sources support an **800-year** tradition of ordered liberty linked to the **Magna Carta (1215)** in **England**, also called the **Great Charter of Freedoms**.

## Core Freedoms and Constitutional Rights
Canadians enjoy key freedoms: **conscience and religion**, **thought and expression** (including speech and press), **peaceful assembly**, and **association**. The common-law principle of **habeas corpus** protects people against unlawful detention by the state. In **1982**, Canada amended its Constitution to entrench the **Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms**, which affirms the **supremacy of God** and the **rule of law**; **Queen Elizabeth II** proclaimed the amended Constitution in **Ottawa (1982)**. This constitutional language also reflects the place of religious traditions and the dignity and worth of every person.

## Rights Highlighted in the Charter
The Charter emphasizes several major protections:
- **Mobility rights**: citizens may live and work anywhere in Canada, leave and re-enter the country, and apply for a passport.
- **Aboriginal Peoples' rights**: Charter rights must not reduce treaty rights or other rights and freedoms of Aboriginal peoples.
- **Official language and minority-language education rights**: **French** and **English** have equal status in **Parliament** and across government.
- **Multiculturalism**: a core part of Canadian heritage and identity, with respect for pluralism and social harmony.

## Equality of Women and Men
In Canada, women and men are equal before and under the law

, women and men are equal before and under the lawCanadian openness does not permit practices such as **spousal abuse**, **honour killings**, **female genital mutilation**, **forced marriage**, or other gender-based violence. These acts are crimes and are punished severely under Canadian criminal law.

## Responsibilities of Citizens
Rights come with clear civic obligations:
- **Obey the law**: the rule of law means people and governments are bound by law, not arbitrary power, and no one is above the law.
- **Support yourself and your family**: work, family care, and effort according to ability build dignity, self-respect, and national prosperity.
- **Serve on a jury when called**: jury service is a legal duty and supports fair, impartial justice.
- **Vote in elections**: citizens should vote in **federal**, **provincial/territorial**, and **local** elections.
- **Help the community**: **millions of volunteers** give time without pay through schools, food banks, charities, and newcomer support, while building useful skills, friendships, and contacts.
- **Protect heritage and the environment**: avoid waste and pollution and protect Canada's natural, cultural, and architectural heritage for future generations.

## Defending and Serving Canada
Canada has **no compulsory military service**, but service in the regular **Canadian Forces** (**navy**, **army**, **air force**) is a respected way to contribute (**www.forces.ca**). Citizens can also serve part-time in local **navy**, **militia**, and **air reserves**, or youth can build discipline, responsibility, and skills through the **cadets** program (**www.cadets.ca**). Public service can also include the **Coast Guard**, local **police forces**, and **fire departments**, continuing the tradition of sacrifice by earlier generations of Canadians.

## Source Note
In the printed guide, this chapter appears across **pages 11 to 15**.",

this chapter appears across **pages 11 to 15**.",    "fr": "## Fondements juridiques de la citoyenneté
La citoyenneté canadienne combine des droits et des devoirs issus de l'histoire, du droit et de valeurs communes. Le droit canadien repose sur le **Parlement**, les **législatures provinciales**, la **common law anglaise**, le **Code civil de France** et une tradition constitutionnelle non écrite héritée de la **Grande-Bretagne**. Ensemble, ces sources soutiennent une tradition de liberté ordonnée vieille d'environ **800 ans**, rattachée à la **Magna Carta (1215)** en **Angleterre**, aussi appelée la **Grande Charte des libertés**.

## Libertés fondamentales et droits constitutionnels
Les Canadiennes et Canadiens bénéficient de libertés essentielles: **liberté de conscience et de religion**, **liberté de pensée et d'expression** (y compris la parole et la presse), **liberté de réunion pacifique** et **liberté d'association**. Le principe de **l'habeas corpus**, hérité de la common law, protège contre la détention illégale par l'État. En **1982**, la Constitution a été modifiée pour y enchâsser la **Charte canadienne des droits et libertés**, qui affirme la **suprématie de Dieu** et la **primauté du droit**; **la reine Elizabeth II** a proclamé la Constitution modifiée à **Ottawa (1982)**

clamé la Constitution modifiée à **Ottawa (1982)**Cette formulation souligne aussi la place des traditions religieuses ainsi que la dignité et la valeur de chaque personne.

## Droits mis en évidence par la Charte
La Charte insiste notamment sur les protections suivantes:
- **Droits à la mobilité**: vivre et travailler partout au Canada, entrer et sortir du pays librement, et demander un passeport.
- **Droits des peuples autochtones**: les garanties de la Charte ne doivent pas porter atteinte aux droits issus de traités ni aux autres droits et libertés des peuples autochtones.
- **Droits linguistiques officiels et droits à l'instruction dans la langue de la minorité**: le **français** et l'**anglais** ont un statut égal au **Parlement** et dans l'ensemble du gouvernement.
- **Multiculturalisme**: élément central du patrimoine et de l'identité du Canada, fondé sur le pluralisme et la vie en harmonie.

## Égalité entre les femmes et les hommes
Au Canada, les femmes et les hommes sont égaux devant la loi. L'ouverture canadienne n'accepte pas des pratiques comme la **violence conjugale**, les **crimes dits d'honneur**, les **mutilations génitales féminines**, le **mariage forcé** ou d'autres formes de violence fondée sur le sexe

ou d'autres formes de violence fondée sur le sexeCes actes sont criminels et sévèrement punis par le droit pénal canadien.

## Responsabilités des citoyens
Les droits s'accompagnent d'obligations civiques claires:
- **Respecter la loi**: la primauté du droit signifie que les personnes et les gouvernements sont soumis à la loi, non à l'arbitraire, et que nul n'est au-dessus de la loi.
- **Assumer ses responsabilités envers soi-même et sa famille**: travailler, prendre soin de sa famille et faire des efforts selon ses capacités favorisent la dignité, le respect de soi et la prospérité du pays.
- **Siéger comme juré lorsqu'on est convoqué**: c'est une obligation légale qui soutient une justice impartiale.
- **Voter aux élections**: il faut participer aux élections **fédérales**, **provinciales/territoriales** et **locales**.
- **Aider sa communauté**: des **millions de bénévoles** donnent du temps sans rémunération dans les écoles, banques alimentaires, organismes de charité et services d'intégration, tout en développant des compétences utiles, des amitiés et des contacts.
- **Protéger le patrimoine et l'environnement**: éviter le gaspillage et la pollution, et préserver le patrimoine naturel, culturel et architectural du Canada pour les générations futures.

## Défendre et servir le Canada
Le Canada n'impose **pas de service militaire obligatoire**, mais servir dans les **Forces canadiennes** régulières (**marine**, **armée**, **aviation**) est une contribution reconnue (**www.forces.ca**). On peut aussi servir à temps partiel dans la **marine**, la **milice** et les **réserves aériennes** locales, et les jeunes peuvent acquérir discipline, sens des responsabilités et compétences dans le programme des **cadets** (**www.cadets.ca**)

ns le programme des **cadets** (**www.cadets.ca**)Le service public comprend aussi la **Garde côtière**, les **services policiers** locaux et les **services d'incendie**, dans la continuité des sacrifices des générations canadiennes précédentes.

## Note de source
Dans l'édition imprimée, ce chapitre se trouve aux **pages 11 à 15**."

"sectionName": "Canada's Regions",
  "content": {    "en": "## Canada’s Geography and Regions
Canada is the **second-largest country**, at about **10 million km²**. It is bordered by the **Pacific Ocean** (west), **Atlantic Ocean** (east), and **Arctic Ocean** (north), with the Canada–U.S. boundary along the south. The guide divides Canada into **five regions**: **Atlantic Provinces**, **Central Canada**, **Prairie Provinces**, **West Coast**, **Northern Territories**.

## Capital, Population, and Capitals Map
**Ottawa** (on the Ottawa River) was chosen as capital in **1857** by **Queen Victoria**, the great-great-grandmother of Queen Elizabeth II. Ottawa is now Canada’s **4th-largest metropolitan area**. The **National Capital Region** covers about **4,700 km²**.

Canada has **10 provinces** and **3 territories**. Population is **34 million**. Capitals:
- Newfoundland and Labrador — **St. John’s**
- Prince Edward Island — **Charlottetown**
- Nova Scotia — **Halifax**
- New Brunswick — **Fredericton**
- Quebec — **Québec City**
- Ontario — **Toronto**
- Manitoba — **Winnipeg**
- Saskatchewan — **Regina**
- Alberta — **Edmonton**
- British Columbia — **Victoria**
- Nunavut — **Iqaluit**
- Northwest Territories — **Yellowknife**
- Yukon Territory — **Whitehorse**

## Atlantic Provinces
Atlantic Canada grew through **fishing, farming, forestry, and mining**

through **fishing, farming, forestry, and mining**Climate: cool winters and cool humid summers.

### Newfoundland and Labrador
- Easternmost point in North America; has its own time zone.
- Maritime heritage; oldest colony of the British Empire.
- Historically known for fisheries and coastal villages.
- Today: offshore oil and gas are major; Labrador has major hydro-electric resources.

### Prince Edward Island
- Canada’s smallest province; known for beaches, red soil, and potatoes.
- Birthplace of Confederation.
- Linked to mainland by the **Confederation Bridge** (one of the longest continuous multispan bridges).
- Literary symbol: **Anne of Green Gables** by **Lucy Maud Montgomery**.

### Nova Scotia
- Most populous Atlantic province; historical gateway to Canada.
- **Bay of Fundy** has the world’s highest tides.
- **Halifax**: largest deep-water, ice-free east coast port; major defence and trade hub; home to Canada’s largest naval base.
- Long history in coal, forestry, agriculture; now also offshore oil and gas exploration.
- Strong Celtic/Gaelic culture; over **700 annual festivals** (including the Halifax military tattoo).

### New Brunswick
- In the Appalachian Range; founded by **United Empire Loyalists**.
- Has the **St. John River system**, the second-largest on North America’s Atlantic coastline.
- Main industries: forestry, agriculture, fisheries, mining, food processing, tourism.
- **Saint John** (largest city/port/manufacturing), **Moncton** (main Acadian Francophone centre), **Fredericton** (historic capital).
- Only officially bilingual province; about **one-third** of residents live and work in French.

## Central Canada
More than **half** of Canadians live near the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence in southern Ontario and Quebec. This is the manufacturing heartland. Climate: cold winters and warm humid summers. Ontario + Quebec produce **more than three-quarters** of Canadian manufactured goods.

### Quebec
- Nearly **8 million** people, mostly near the St

n- Nearly **8 million** people, mostly near the StLawrence.
- More than **three-quarters** have French as first language.
- Canadian Shield resources support forestry, energy, and mining.
- Canada’s main producer of pulp and paper; largest producer of hydro-electricity.
- Strong advanced sectors: pharmaceuticals and aeronautics.
- Cultural exports (film, music, literature, food) have global reach, especially in **La Francophonie**.
- **Montreal**: Canada’s 2nd-largest city; world’s 2nd-largest mainly French-speaking city after Paris.

### Ontario
- More than **12 million** people (over one-third of Canadians).
- **Toronto**: largest city and main financial centre.
- Services/manufacturing drive a large share of exports.
- **Niagara**: vineyards, wines, fruit crops.
- Farming includes dairy, beef, poultry, vegetables, grains.
- Founded by United Empire Loyalists; has the largest French-speaking population outside Quebec.
- Great Lakes listed: **Ontario, Erie, Huron, Michigan (in U.S.A.), Superior** (largest freshwater lake in the world).

## Prairie Provinces
**Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta**: rich energy resources plus highly fertile farmland. Regional climate is generally dry, with long cold winters and hot summers.

### Manitoba
- Economy: agriculture, mining, hydro-electric generation.
- **Winnipeg** includes **Portage and Main** (famous intersection).
- **St

*Portage and Main** (famous intersection).
- **StBoniface**: largest Francophone community in Western Canada (**45,000**).
- Important Ukrainian centre (**14%** report Ukrainian origins).
- Largest Aboriginal provincial population share (over **15%**).

### Saskatchewan
- Formerly “breadbasket of the world” and “wheat province.”
- About **40%** of Canada’s arable land; largest producer of grains and oilseeds.
- World-rich deposits of uranium and potash (fertilizer), plus oil and gas.
- **Regina**: RCMP training academy.
- **Saskatoon**: largest city; major mining, education, research, and technology centre.

### Alberta
- Most populous Prairie province.
- Alberta and **Lake Louise** named after **Princess Louise Caroline Alberta** (Queen Victoria’s fourth daughter).
- **Five national parks**, including **Banff (1885)**.
- **Badlands**: major prehistoric fossil/dinosaur finds.
- Largest producer of oil and gas; northern oil sands developed as an energy source.
- Major cattle ranching and beef production region.

## West Coast: British Columbia
**B.C.** is Canada’s westernmost province (about **4 million** people). The **Port of Vancouver** is Canada’s largest/busiest port and a gateway to the **Asia-Pacific**, handling billions in trade. Pacific air currents create a temperate coastal climate.

About half of B.C. output is forestry (lumber, newsprint, pulp and paper), the country’s most valuable forestry industry. Other strengths: mining, fishing, and Okanagan orchards/wine. B.C. has about **600 provincial parks** (Canada’s largest provincial park system). After English, **Chinese** and **Punjabi** are widely spoken in cities. **Victoria** is the capital, a tourism centre, and headquarters of the navy’s Pacific fleet.

## Northern Territories
**Yukon, Northwest Territories, Nunavut** cover about **one-third** of Canada’s land mass but have about **100,000** people

nada’s land mass but have about **100,000** peopleThe region has mines (gold, lead, copper, diamond, zinc) and developing oil/gas projects.

The North is called the “**Land of the Midnight Sun**”: up to **24 hours** of summer daylight; around **three months** of winter darkness. Much of the land is tundra (rocky Arctic plain), treeless, with permanently frozen ground. Some residents continue hunting, fishing, and trapping. **Inuit art** is sold across Canada and internationally.

### Yukon
- Gold Rush of the **1890s** drew thousands of miners; celebrated in poetry by **Robert W. Service**.
- Mining remains significant.
- **White Pass and Yukon Railway** opened in **1900** from **Skagway (Alaska)** to **Whitehorse**.
- Coldest recorded Canadian temperature: **-63°C**.
- **Mount Logan** (Yukon) is Canada’s highest mountain; named for **Sir William Logan** (born **Montreal, 1798**), founder/director of the **Geological Survey of Canada (1842–1869)**.

### Northwest Territories
- Formed in **1870** from **Rupert’s Land** and the **North-Western Territory**.
- **Yellowknife** (about **20,000**) is called the “diamond capital of North America.”
- More than half the population is Aboriginal (**Dene, Inuit, Métis**).
- **Mackenzie River system**: about **4,200 km**, second longest in North America after the Mississippi; drains about **1.8 million km²**.

### Nunavut
- **Nunavut** means “our land” in **Inuktitut**.
- Created in **1999** from eastern N.W.T

titut**.
- Created in **1999** from eastern N.W.T(including former District of Keewatin).
- Capital: **Iqaluit** (formerly **Frobisher Bay**), named after explorer **Martin Frobisher**, who reached the Arctic for **Queen Elizabeth I** in **1576**.
- **19-member Legislative Assembly** chooses premier and ministers by consensus.
- Population is about **85% Inuit**.
- **Inuktitut** is an official language and the first language of schooling.

## Canadian Rangers
The chapter describes northern sovereignty/security challenges and the role of the **Canadian Rangers** (part of the **Canadian Forces Reserves/militia**). Using Indigenous knowledge, Rangers patrol by snowmobile in winter and all-terrain vehicles in summer, from **Resolute** toward the **Magnetic North Pole**, maintaining Canadian presence in the Arctic.

Image notes referenced in the chapter include: **Rideau Canal** (former military waterway, now tourist site and winter skateway), **Peggy’s Cove** harbour, and **caribou** as both game and a northern symbol.",

**caribou** as both game and a northern symbol.",    "fr": "## Géographie et régions du Canada
Le Canada, **deuxième plus grand pays**, couvre **10 millions de km²**. Trois océans le bordent: **Pacifique** (ouest), **Atlantique** (est), **Arctique** (nord). Au sud, frontière avec les États-Unis.

Le guide distingue **5 régions** :
- **Provinces de l’Atlantique**
- **Canada central**
- **Provinces des Prairies**
- **Côte Ouest**
- **Territoires du Nord**

## Capitale, population et capitales
**Ottawa**, sur la rivière des Outaouais, devient capitale en **1857** par choix de **la reine Victoria** (arrière-arrière-grand-mère de la reine Elizabeth II). Ottawa est aujourd’hui la **4e région métropolitaine** du pays. La **Région de la capitale nationale** couvre environ **4 700 km²**.

Le Canada compte **10 provinces** et **3 territoires**. Population: **34 millions**.

Capitales à connaître:
- Terre-Neuve-et-Labrador — **St. John’s**
- Île-du-Prince-Édouard — **Charlottetown**
- Nouvelle-Écosse — **Halifax**
- Nouveau-Brunswick — **Fredericton**
- Québec — **Québec**
- Ontario — **Toronto**
- Manitoba — **Winnipeg**
- Saskatchewan — **Regina**
- Alberta — **Edmonton**
- Colombie-Britannique — **Victoria**
- Nunavut — **Iqaluit**
- Territoires du Nord-Ouest — **Yellowknife**
- Yukon — **Whitehorse**

## Provinces de l’Atlantique
Région marquée par le littoral et les ressources: **pêche, agriculture, foresterie, mines**

sources: **pêche, agriculture, foresterie, mines**Climat: hivers frais, étés frais et humides.

### Terre-Neuve-et-Labrador
- Point le plus à l’est de l’Amérique du Nord; fuseau horaire propre.
- Héritage maritime; plus ancienne colonie de l’Empire britannique.
- Pêches et villages côtiers historiquement centraux.
- Aujourd’hui: pétrole/gaz extracôtiers + fort potentiel hydroélectrique au Labrador.

### Île-du-Prince-Édouard
- Plus petite province.
- Connue pour plages, sol rouge, pommes de terre.
- Berceau de la Confédération.
- Reliée au continent par le **pont de la Confédération** (long pont continu à travées multiples).
- Référence littéraire: **Lucy Maud Montgomery**, *Anne… la maison aux pignons verts*.

### Nouvelle-Écosse
- Province atlantique la plus peuplée; porte d’entrée du Canada.
- **Baie de Fundy**: marées les plus hautes du monde.
- **Halifax**: plus grand port de la côte est (eau profonde, libre de glace), centre de commerce et de défense, plus grande base navale du pays.
- Économie: construction navale, pêches, transport maritime, charbon, foresterie, agriculture, plus exploration pétrolière/gazière extracôtière.
- Culture **celtique/gaélique**; plus de **700 festivals** annuels (dont le tatouage militaire de Halifax).

### Nouveau-Brunswick
- Dans les **Appalaches**; fondé par les **Loyalistes de l’Empire-Uni**.
- **Système du fleuve Saint-Jean**: deuxième grand système fluvial de la côte atlantique nord-américaine.
- Industries: foresterie, agriculture, pêche, mines, transformation alimentaire, tourisme.
- **Saint John** (plus grande ville/port/centre manufacturier), **Moncton** (principal centre acadien francophone), **Fredericton** (capitale historique).
- Seule province **bilingue**; **1/3** de la population vit et travaille en français.

## Canada central
Plus de **la moitié** des Canadiens vivent près des **Grands Lacs** et du **Saint-Laurent** (sud Ontario/Québec). Cœur industriel et manufacturier du pays. Climat: hivers froids, étés chauds et humides

ays. Climat: hivers froids, étés chauds et humidesOntario + Québec produisent **plus des 3/4** des biens manufacturés canadiens.

### Québec
- Près de **8 millions** d’habitants, surtout le long du Saint-Laurent.
- Plus des **3/4** ont le français comme première langue.
- Ressources du **Bouclier canadien**: foresterie, énergie, mines.
- Principal producteur canadien de **pâtes et papiers**; plus grand producteur d’**hydroélectricité**.
- Secteurs de pointe: **pharmaceutique** et **aéronautique**.
- Rayonnement culturel international, surtout dans la **Francophonie**.
- **Montréal**: 2e ville du Canada; 2e grande ville majoritairement francophone après Paris.

### Ontario
- Plus de **12 millions** d’habitants (plus du tiers des Canadiens).
- **Toronto**: plus grande ville et principal centre financier.
- Services + fabrication = part importante des exportations.
- **Niagara**: vignobles, vins, fruits.
- Agriculture: bovins laitiers/de boucherie, volaille, légumes, céréales.
- Héritage des Loyalistes; plus grande population francophone hors Québec.
- Grands Lacs cités: **Ontario, Érié, Huron, Michigan (États-Unis), Supérieur** (plus grand lac d’eau douce du monde).

## Provinces des Prairies
**Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta**: ressources énergétiques + terres très fertiles

**: ressources énergétiques + terres très fertilesClimat sec, hivers longs et froids, étés chauds.

### Manitoba
- Économie: agriculture, mines, hydroélectricité.
- **Winnipeg**: quartier Exchange avec **Portage et Main**.
- **Saint-Boniface**: plus grande communauté francophone de l’Ouest (**45 000**).
- Culture ukrainienne importante (**14 %** d’origines ukrainiennes).
- Plus forte proportion autochtone provinciale (plus de **15 %**).

### Saskatchewan
- Ancien « grenier du monde » / « province du blé ».
- Environ **40 %** des terres arables canadiennes; premier producteur de céréales et d’oléagineux.
- Très riches gisements d’**uranium** et de **potasse**; production de pétrole et de gaz.
- **Regina**: académie de formation de la **GRC**.
- **Saskatoon**: plus grande ville; pôle minier, éducation, recherche, technologies.

### Alberta
- Province des Prairies la plus peuplée.
- Nom de la province et de **Lake Louise** lié à **la princesse Louise Caroline Alberta** (4e fille de la reine Victoria).
- **5 parcs nationaux**, dont **Banff (1885)**.
- **Badlands**: grands gisements de fossiles et découvertes de dinosaures.
- Premier producteur canadien de pétrole et de gaz; développement des sables bitumineux du nord.
- Province majeure pour l’élevage bovin et le bœuf.

## Côte Ouest: Colombie-Britannique
La **C.-B.** est la province la plus à l’ouest (**4 millions** d’habitants). Le **port de Vancouver** est le plus grand et le plus actif du Canada, porte vers l’**Asie-Pacifique**. Le littoral bénéficie d’un climat tempéré.

Environ la moitié de la production de la C.-B. vient de la foresterie (bois d’œuvre, papier journal, pâtes et papiers). Autres atouts: mines, pêche, vergers et vins de l’**Okanagan**. La C.-B. compte **600 parcs provinciaux**. Après l’anglais, le **chinois** et le **pendjabi** sont très parlés en ville

ois** et le **pendjabi** sont très parlés en ville**Victoria** est la capitale, centre touristique et quartier général de la flotte du Pacifique.

## Territoires du Nord
**Yukon**, **T.N.-O.** et **Nunavut** couvrent le **tiers** de la superficie canadienne, avec **100 000** habitants. Mines d’or, plomb, cuivre, diamant, zinc; projets pétroliers et gaziers.

Le Nord est le « **pays du soleil de minuit** »: jusqu’à **24 h** de clarté l’été, puis environ **3 mois** d’obscurité l’hiver. Grande présence de **toundra** (plaine arctique rocheuse), sans arbres, avec pergélisol. La chasse, la pêche et le piégeage restent présents. L’**art inuit** est vendu partout.

### Yukon
- Ruée vers l’or des **années 1890**; poésie de **Robert W. Service**.
- Les mines restent importantes.
- **White Pass and Yukon Railway** ouvert en **1900** entre **Skagway (Alaska)** et **Whitehorse**.
- Record de froid canadien: **-63 °C**.
- **Mont Logan**: plus haut sommet du Canada; nommé pour **sir William Logan** (né à **Montréal en 1798**), fondateur/directeur de la **Commission géologique du Canada (1842–1869)**.

### Territoires du Nord-Ouest
- Constitués en **1870** à partir de la **Terre de Rupert** et du **Territoire du Nord-Ouest**.
- **Yellowknife** (env. **20 000**): « capitale du diamant de l’Amérique du Nord ».
- Plus de la moitié de la population est autochtone (**Dénés, Inuits, Métis**).
- Système du **Mackenzie**: env. **4 200 km**, 2e plus long d’Amérique du Nord après le Mississippi; bassin d’env. **1,8 million km²**.

### Nunavut
- **Nunavut** signifie « notre terre » en **inuktitut**.
- Créé en **1999** à partir de l’est des T.N.-O

.
- Créé en **1999** à partir de l’est des T.N.-O(incluant l’ancien district de Keewatin).
- Capitale: **Iqaluit** (ancien **Frobisher Bay**), d’après **Martin Frobisher**, explorateur de l’Arctique pour **la reine Elizabeth Ire** en **1576**.
- **Assemblée législative de 19 membres** choisissant premier ministre et ministres par consensus.
- Population: **85 %** inuit.
- **Inuktitut**: langue officielle et première langue scolaire.

## Rangers canadiens
Le Nord pose des enjeux de souveraineté/sécurité. Les **Rangers canadiens** (dans la **Réserve des Forces canadiennes / milice**) jouent un rôle clé. Avec les savoirs autochtones, ils patrouillent en **motoneige** l’hiver et en **véhicules tout-terrain** l’été, de **Resolute** vers le **pôle Nord magnétique**."

"sectionName": "Study Questions",
  "content": {    "en": "## How This Chapter Prepares You
This section explains that showing **adequate knowledge of Canada** is a core citizenship requirement. The **citizenship test** checks your understanding of Canada and of your **rights and responsibilities** as a citizen. The guide states that every test question comes from this study book, so you are tested on facts and ideas already presented here.

## Sample Question Patterns
The sample multiple-choice items train you to separate correct civic duties from distractors. For responsibilities, the correct set is **obeying the law**, **taking responsibility for yourself and your family**, and **serving on a jury**. Other options (for example references to recycling newspapers, joining a union, buying Canadian products, or serving in the navy/army/air force) are included to test judgment, not memorization alone.

You are also asked to interpret symbols and institutions. The **Remembrance Day poppy** stands for remembering Canadians who **served or died in wars up to the present day**

o **served or died in wars up to the present day**Members of **Parliament (MPs)** are chosen by voters in a local **constituency (riding)**, not by the **United Nations**, provincial premiers, landowners, or police chiefs.

## Study Checklist by Topic

- **rights, freedoms, and equality** (including equality of women and men, and key rights documents)
- **citizenship responsibilities** and family/self-reliance

  "sectionName": "Who We Are",
  "content": {    "en": "## National Identity and Founding Peoples
Canada is presented as a **strong and free country** with a distinct identity. The text says Canadians inherited the **oldest continuous constitutional tradition in the world**, and that Canada is the only **constitutional monarchy in North America**. Its institutions are tied to **Peace, Order, and Good Government** in the **British North America Act (1867)**, and to values such as ordered liberty, enterprise, hard work, and fair play. From the **Atlantic** to the **Pacific Ocean** and the **Arctic Circle**, the country is sometimes praised as the **Great Dominion**, and its story is framed through three founding peoples: **Aboriginal**, **French**, and **British**.

## Aboriginal Peoples: Origins, Rights, and Renewal
The ancestors of Aboriginal peoples are believed to have migrated from **Asia** thousands of years before Europeans arrived. Their cultures were diverse and rooted in beliefs about the **Creator**, the natural world, and community ties. **Aboriginal and treaty rights** are recognized in the Constitution, and the **Royal Proclamation of 1763** by **King George III** first guaranteed territorial rights and created a basis for treaties, though many treaties were not fully respected. From the **1800s to the 1980s**, many children were sent to residential schools for assimilation; languages and cultural practices were largely banned, many students suffered abuse, and in **2008** the federal government in **Ottawa** formally apologized.

## First Nations, Inuit, and Metis
The chapter identifies three distinct Aboriginal groups:
- **First Nations**: "Indian" refers to Aboriginal people who are not Inuit or Metis; since the **1970s**, "First Nations" became common usage

e **1970s**, "First Nations" became common usageAbout half live on reserve land in about **600 communities**, and about half live off-reserve, mainly in urban centres.
- **Inuit**: in **Inuktitut**, Inuit means "the people." Inuit communities are small and spread across the Arctic, and their knowledge of land, sea, and wildlife helped them adapt to one of the harshest environments on earth.
- **Metis**: a distinct people of mixed Aboriginal and European ancestry, mostly in the **Prairie provinces**, with French- and English-speaking roots and their own dialect, **Michif**.
The chapter gives approximate proportions: **65% First Nations**, **30% Metis**, and **4% Inuit**. It also notes renewed Aboriginal pride and major achievements in **agriculture**, the **environment**, **business**, and the **arts**.

## Unity in Diversity
**John Buchan (1st Baron Tweedsmuir)**, Governor General from **1935 to 1940**, said in a **1937** speech at the **Canadian Club of Halifax** that immigrant groups should keep their individuality while also building shared loyalty to Canada. The chapter also references the **15th Governor General** shown in a **Blood (Kainai First Nation)** headdress.

## English and French Foundations
Modern Canada is described as deeply shaped by English- and French-speaking Christian civilizations from Europe. **English** and **French** define daily life for most people and are the official languages; by law, the federal government must provide services across Canada in both. The chapter cites about **18 million Anglophones** and **seven million Francophones**. Most Francophones live in **Quebec**, while about **one million** live in **Ontario**, **New Brunswick**, and **Manitoba**, with smaller communities in other provinces; **New Brunswick** is the only officially bilingual province.

## Acadians, Quebecers, and Anglo-Quebecers
**Acadians** descend from French colonists who began settling in the Maritimes in **1604**

ts who began settling in the Maritimes in **1604**During the Britain-France war from **1755 to 1763**, more than two-thirds were deported in the **Great Upheaval**, but Acadian identity survived and remains vibrant. The chapter says most Quebecers descend from about **8,500** French settlers from the **1600s and 1700s**, and that the **House of Commons** recognized in **2006** that the **Quebecois** form a nation within a united Canada. It also notes about **one million Anglo-Quebecers** with a heritage of roughly **250 years**.

## Becoming Canadian
Some newcomers come from countries affected by war or conflict, but the text says this cannot justify importing violent, extreme, or hateful prejudices. New Canadians are expected to embrace democratic principles, especially the **rule of law**. The English-speaking way of life was shaped by hundreds of thousands of **English**, **Welsh**, **Scottish**, and **Irish** settlers, soldiers, and migrants from the **1600s to the 20th century**. This history helps explain why many Anglophones are called **English Canadians**.

## Diversity in Canada Today
Most Canadians were born in Canada, and this has been true since the **1800s**, yet Canada is also described as a land of immigration because over the last **200 years** millions of newcomers helped build and defend the country. Many ethnic and religious communities live and work in peace as proud Canadians. The largest groups listed are **English, French, Scottish, Irish, German, Italian, Chinese, Aboriginal, Ukrainian, Dutch, South Asian,** and **Scandinavian**; since the **1970s**, most immigrants have come from **Asia**

**1970s**, most immigrants have come from **Asia**The chapter adds that non-official languages are widely spoken at home, with Chinese languages second after English in major cities (**13%** in **Vancouver**, **7%** in **Toronto**).

## Religion, Equality, and Cultural Examples
The great majority of Canadians identify as Christians, with **Catholic** affiliation largest, followed by **Protestant** churches; populations of **Muslims, Jews, Hindus, Sikhs**, other religions, and people with **no religion** are also growing. The state has traditionally partnered with faith communities to support social welfare, harmony, schools, health care, refugee resettlement, and freedom of religion, religious expression, and conscience. The chapter also states that **gay and lesbian Canadians** receive full legal protection and equal treatment, including access to **civil marriage**.

## Named Events, Places, and People
Examples named in the chapter include **St. Patrick's Day Parade (Montreal, Quebec)**, **Glengarry Highland Games (Maxville, Ontario)**, **Fete Nationale (Gatineau, Quebec)**, and an **Acadian fiddler in Grande-Anse, New Brunswick**. It also references **Celebration of Cultures (Edmonton, Alberta)**, **Ismaili Muslims in the Calgary Stampede**, a **Caribbean cultural festival (Toronto, Ontario)**, **Ukrainian Pysanka Festival (Vegreville, Alberta)**, and young Polish dancers in **Oliver, British Columbia**, plus pipes and drums in **Ottawa**. Other captions mention **Metis from Alberta**, a **Cree dancer**, **Inuit children in Iqaluit, Nunavut**, and **Haida artist Bill Reid**, as well as **Christmas in Gatineau**, **Chinese-Canadian war veterans**, **Notre-Dame-des-Victoires (Quebec City)**, and **Chinese New Year (Vancouver)**

ebec City)**, and **Chinese New Year (Vancouver)**A final example highlights Olympian **Marjorie Turner-Bailey** of **Nova Scotia**, linked to **Black Loyalists**, escaped slaves, and free men and women of African origin who fled in the **1780s** from the United States, where slavery remained legal until **1863**.

## Source Note
In the printed guide, this chapter appears across **pages 16 to 22**.",

this chapter appears across **pages 16 to 22**.",    "fr": "## Identite nationale et peuples fondateurs
Le Canada est presente comme un **pays fort et libre** avec une identite propre. Le texte affirme que les Canadiens ont herite de la **plus ancienne tradition constitutionnelle continue au monde**, et que le Canada est la seule **monarchie constitutionnelle en Amerique du Nord**. Ses institutions renvoient a **Paix, ordre et bon gouvernement** dans la **Loi constitutionnelle de 1867** (anciennement l'Acte de l'Amerique du Nord britannique), ainsi qu'a des valeurs de liberte ordonnee, d'esprit d'entreprise, de travail et de fair-play. De l'**Atlantique** au **Pacifique** jusqu'au **cercle arctique**, le pays est parfois celebre comme le **Grand Dominion**, et son histoire est presentee a travers trois peuples fondateurs: **autochtones**, **francais** et **britanniques**.

## Peuples autochtones: origines, droits et renouveau
Les ancetres des peuples autochtones seraient venus d'**Asie** des milliers d'annees avant l'arrivee des Europeens. Leurs cultures etaient diverses et ancrees dans des croyances liees au **Createur**, a la nature et a la communaute. Les **droits autochtones et issus de traites** sont reconnus dans la Constitution, et la **Proclamation royale de 1763** du **roi George III** a d'abord garanti des droits territoriaux et etabli la base des traites, meme si plusieurs n'ont pas ete pleinement respectes. Des **annees 1800 aux annees 1980**, de nombreux enfants ont ete places dans des pensionnats pour l'assimilation; les langues et pratiques culturelles etaient largement interdites, des eleves ont subi des abus, puis des excuses officielles ont ete presentees a **Ottawa** en **2008**.

## Premieres Nations, Inuit et Metis
Le chapitre distingue trois groupes autochtones:
- **Premieres Nations**: le terme "Indien" vise les Autochtones qui ne sont ni Inuit ni Metis; depuis les **annees 1970**, l'expression "Premieres Nations" s'est generalisee

expression "Premieres Nations" s'est generaliseeEnviron la moitie vit sur des reserves dans pres de **600 collectivites**, et l'autre moitie vit hors reserve, surtout en milieu urbain.
- **Inuit**: en **inuktitut**, Inuit signifie "le peuple". Les collectivites inuit sont petites et dispersees dans l'Arctique, et leur connaissance de la terre, de la mer et de la faune a permis l'adaptation a un environnement parmi les plus rudes.
- **Metis**: peuple distinct d'ascendance mixte autochtone et europeenne, majoritairement present dans les **provinces des Prairies**, avec des racines francophones et anglophones et un parler propre, le **michif**.
Le texte donne des proportions approximatives: **65 % Premieres Nations**, **30 % Metis** et **4 % Inuit**. Il souligne aussi le renouveau de la fierte autochtone et des reussites importantes en **agriculture**, en **environnement**, en **affaires** et dans les **arts**.

## Unite dans la diversite
**John Buchan (1er baron Tweedsmuir)**, gouverneur general de **1935 a 1940**, declare dans un discours de **1937** au **Canadian Club of Halifax** que les groupes immigrants doivent conserver leur individualite tout en construisant une loyaute commune envers le Canada. Le chapitre mentionne aussi le **15e gouverneur general** portant une coiffe des **Blood (Premiere Nation Kainai)**.

## Fondements anglais et francais
Le Canada moderne est decrit comme fortement marque par les civilisations chretiennes anglophone et francophone venues d'Europe. Le **francais** et l'**anglais** structurent la vie quotidienne de la majorite et sont les langues officielles; en vertu de la loi, le gouvernement federal doit offrir des services partout au pays dans les deux langues. Le chapitre cite environ **18 millions d'anglophones** et **sept millions de francophones**

anglophones** et **sept millions de francophones**La majorite des francophones vit au **Quebec**, alors qu'environ **un million** vivent en **Ontario**, au **Nouveau-Brunswick** et au **Manitoba**, avec des presences plus petites ailleurs; le **Nouveau-Brunswick** est la seule province officiellement bilingue.

## Acadiens, Quebecois et Anglo-Quebecois
Les **Acadiens** descendent de colons francais installes dans les Maritimes a partir de **1604**. Pendant la guerre entre la Grande-Bretagne et la France, de **1755 a 1763**, plus des deux tiers ont ete deportes lors du **Grand Derangement**, mais l'identite acadienne a survecu et demeure vivante. Le chapitre indique que la plupart des Quebecois descendent d'environ **8 500** colons francais des **XVIIe et XVIIIe siecles**, et que la **Chambre des communes** a reconnu en **2006** que les **Quebecois** forment une nation au sein d'un Canada uni. Il mentionne aussi environ **un million d'Anglo-Quebecois**, avec un heritage d'environ **250 ans**.

## Devenir canadien
Certains nouveaux arrivants viennent de pays touches par la guerre ou les conflits, mais le texte precise que cela ne justifie pas d'importer des prejuges violents, extremistes ou haineux. Les nouveaux Canadiens doivent adherer aux principes democratiques, surtout la **primauté du droit**. Le mode de vie anglophone a ete etabli par des centaines de milliers de colons, soldats et migrants **anglais, gallois, ecossais et irlandais**, du **XVIIe au XXe siecle**. Cette histoire explique pourquoi de nombreux anglophones sont appeles **Canadiens anglais**.

## Diversite du Canada actuel
La majorite des Canadiens sont nes au pays, et cela est vrai depuis les **annees 1800**, mais le Canada est aussi decrit comme une terre d'immigration, car au cours des **200 dernieres annees**, des millions de nouveaux arrivants ont aide a construire et a defendre le pays. De nombreuses communautes ethniques et religieuses vivent et travaillent en paix

iques et religieuses vivent et travaillent en paixLes groupes les plus importants cites sont: **anglais, francais, ecossais, irlandais, allemands, italiens, chinois, autochtones, ukrainiens, neerlandais, sud-asiatiques** et **scandinaves**; depuis les **annees 1970**, la majorite des immigrants viennent d'**Asie**. Le chapitre ajoute que les langues non officielles sont tres presentes a la maison, et que les langues chinoises arrivent au deuxieme rang apres l'anglais dans de grandes villes (**13 %** a **Vancouver**, **7 %** a **Toronto**).

## Religion, egalite et exemples culturels
La grande majorite des Canadiens se disent chretiens, avec les **catholiques** en premier, suivis de diverses Eglises **protestantes**; les populations **musulmanes, juives, hindoues, sikhes**, d'autres religions et des personnes sans religion augmentent aussi. L'Etat collabore depuis longtemps avec les communautes de foi pour le bien-etre social, l'harmonie, les ecoles, les soins de sante, la reinstallation des refugies et la protection de la liberte de religion, d'expression religieuse et de conscience. Le chapitre precise egalement que les **Canadiens gais et lesbiennes** beneficient d'une pleine protection juridique et d'une egalite de traitement, y compris l'acces au **mariage civil**.

## Evenements, lieux et personnes cites
Parmi les exemples nommes: **defile de la Saint-Patrick (Montreal, Quebec)**, **Glengarry Highland Games (Maxville, Ontario)**, **Fete Nationale (Gatineau, Quebec)** et un **violoneux acadien a Grande-Anse (Nouveau-Brunswick)**. Le texte mentionne aussi **Celebration of Cultures (Edmonton, Alberta)**, des **musulmans ismailis au Calgary Stampede**, un **festival culturel caribeen (Toronto, Ontario)**, le **Ukrainian Pysanka Festival (Vegreville, Alberta)** et de jeunes danseurs polonais a **Oliver (Colombie-Britannique)**, ainsi que des cornemuses et tambours a **Ottawa**

ainsi que des cornemuses et tambours a **Ottawa**D'autres vignettes citent des **Metis de l'Alberta**, une **danseuse crie**, des **enfants inuit a Iqaluit (Nunavut)** et l'artiste haida **Bill Reid**, ainsi que **Noel a Gatineau**, des **anciens combattants sino-canadiens**, **Notre-Dame-des-Victoires (Quebec)** et le **Nouvel An chinois (Vancouver)**. Un dernier exemple souligne l'olympienne **Marjorie Turner-Bailey** de **Nouvelle-Ecosse**, reliee aux **Loyalistes noirs**, a des esclaves fugitifs et a des personnes libres d'origine africaine qui ont fui vers le Canada dans les **annees 1780** depuis les Etats-Unis, ou l'esclavage est demeure legal jusqu'en **1863**.

## Note de source
Dans l'edition imprimee, ce chapitre se trouve aux **pages 16 a 22**."

"sectionName": "Canada's History",
  "content": {    "en": "## Indigenous Peoples and Early Contact
Before European arrival, all regions were occupied by Indigenous nations that Europeans called "Indians" because early explorers thought they had reached the East Indies. Ways of life varied: **Huron-Wendat** and **Iroquois** farmed and hunted around the Great Lakes, **Cree** and **Dene** in the Northwest relied on hunting and gathering, **Sioux** followed bison herds, **Inuit** lived from Arctic wildlife, and West Coast peoples preserved fish by drying and smoking. Warfare existed between groups over land, resources, and prestige. European traders, missionaries, soldiers, and settlers transformed this world; disease killed many Indigenous people, but over roughly the first **200 years** of coexistence, Indigenous and Europeans also built strong economic, military, and religious alliances that helped shape Canada.

## First Europeans and Naming Canada
About **1,000 years** ago, Vikings from Icelandic Greenland reached **Labrador** and **Newfoundland**; **l'Anse aux Meadows** is now a World Heritage site. In **1497**, **John Cabot** mapped Canada's Atlantic coast, landed on **Newfoundland or Cape Breton Island**, and claimed the "New Founde Land" for England; permanent English settlement began only in **1610**. Between **1534 and 1542**, **Jacques Cartier** made three voyages for **King Francis I of France**, explored the **St. Lawrence River**, and saw present-day **Quebec City** and **Montreal**. After hearing the Iroquoian word **kanata** (village), mapmakers began using "Canada" in the **1550s**.

## New France and the Continental Struggle
In **1604**, **Pierre de Monts** and **Samuel de Champlain** founded the first European settlement north of Florida at **St. Croix Island** (now in Maine), then **Port-Royal** in **Acadia** (now Nova Scotia)

hen **Port-Royal** in **Acadia** (now Nova Scotia)In **1608**, Champlain built a fortified post at modern **Quebec City** and allied with **Algonquin**, **Montagnais**, and **Huron** against the **Iroquois Confederacy** (five, later six, nations). Peace with the Iroquois came in **1701**. The fur trade, driven by beaver pelts, expanded under leaders like **Jean Talon**, **Bishop Laval**, and **Count Frontenac**, with French influence stretching from **Hudson Bay** to the **Gulf of Mexico**.

## Britain, Quebec, and Loyalist Migration
In **1670**, **King Charles II** gave the **Hudson's Bay Company** monopoly rights over the Hudson Bay watershed. For about **100 years**, its network competed with Montreal traders; canoe men known as **voyageurs** and **coureurs des bois** built strong ties with First Nations. As English Atlantic colonies grew richer, Britain and France fought for control. In **1759**, Britain won the **Battle of the Plains of Abraham** at Quebec City; both commanders, **James Wolfe** and **Marquis de Montcalm**, were killed, ending France's continental empire.

After the war, Britain renamed the colony the **Province of Quebec**. The **Quebec Act (1774)**, a constitutional foundation of Canada, protected Catholic worship, allowed Catholics to hold office, restored **French civil law**, and kept **British criminal law**. When the **13 colonies** declared independence in **1776**, over **40,000 Loyalists** moved north to Nova Scotia and Quebec. **Joseph Brant** brought thousands of Loyalist Mohawk. Loyalists came from Dutch, German, British, Scandinavian, Indigenous, and other backgrounds, and from Presbyterian, Anglican, Baptist, Methodist, Jewish, Quaker, and Catholic traditions

Methodist, Jewish, Quaker, and Catholic traditionsAbout **3,000 Black Loyalists** (freed and enslaved people) also arrived; in **1792**, some Black Nova Scotians later relocated to found **Freetown, Sierra Leone**.

## Democracy and the End of Slavery
Representative government developed step by step: elected assembly in **Halifax (1758)**, then **Prince Edward Island (1773)** and **New Brunswick (1785)**. The **Constitutional Act (1791)** split Quebec into **Upper Canada** (later Ontario) and **Lower Canada** (later Quebec), each with elected legislatures; "Canada" became an official political name, and the colonies were collectively called **British North America**. In **Lower Canada**, the first elected assembly debated the use of both French and English on **January 21, 1793**.

Upper Canada, led by **Lieutenant Governor John Graves Simcoe**, became in **1793** the first province in the Empire to move toward abolition. Britain banned slave trading in **1807** and abolished slavery across the Empire in **1833**. Many enslaved people fled the U.S. through the **Underground Railroad**. **Mary Ann (Shadd) Carey** became Canada's first woman newspaper publisher in **1853**, co-founding and editing **The Provincial Freeman**, which promoted anti-slavery, Black immigration, temperance, and continued British connection.

## Economy and the War of 1812
Early Canadian companies grew from fur-trade competition. Financial institutions appeared in the late 1700s and early 1800s, and the **Montreal Stock Exchange** opened in **1832**. For centuries, the economy depended mainly on farming and exports of fur, fish, and timber moved by roads, lakes, rivers, and canals.

After **Trafalgar (1805)**, tensions with the U.S. led to invasion in **June 1812**. Canadian volunteers, British troops, and First Nations including **Shawnee under Chief Tecumseh** defended the colonies. **Sir Isaac Brock** captured **Detroit** in July but died at **Queenston Heights**

Ross later died and was buried in Halifax with military honours. Naval fighting included **HMS Shannon** bringing captured **USS Chesapeake** into Halifax (1813).

By **1814**, U.S. conquest failed. Britain then funded major defences: citadels at **Halifax** and **Quebec City**, Halifax naval drydock, and **Fort Henry** at Kingston. ## Rebellions, Responsible Government, and Confederation
Rebellions in **1837-38** near Montreal and Toronto failed for lack of broad support; rebels were defeated, and some were hanged or exiled. **Lord Durham** recommended union of Upper and Lower Canada plus responsible government, meaning ministers must keep confidence of elected representatives. He also wrongly promoted assimilation of French Canadians. Reform leaders such as **Sir Etienne-Paschal Tache**, **Sir George-Etienne Cartier**, and **Sir John A. Macdonald** later became Fathers of Confederation.

In **1840**, Upper and Lower Canada were united as the **Province of Canada**. Reformers **La Fontaine**, **Robert Baldwin**, and **Joseph Howe** pushed responsible government. **Nova Scotia (1847-48)** was first to achieve full responsible government; in **1848-49**, **Lord Elgin** implemented it in United Canada

**, **Lord Elgin** implemented it in United Canada**Sir Louis-Hippolyte La Fontaine** became the first head of responsible government in the Canadas and a major defender of French-language rights.

From **1864 to 1867**, Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and the Province of Canada negotiated Confederation with British support. The **British North America Act (1867)** created federal and provincial governments, split the old Province of Canada into **Ontario** and **Quebec**, and formed the **Dominion of Canada** with New Brunswick and Nova Scotia on **July 1, 1867**. The date was called **Dominion Day** until **1982**, then officially **Canada Day**.

## Dominion Expansion and National Builders
In **1864**, **Sir Leonard Tilley** proposed "Dominion of Canada," inspired by **Psalm 72** ("from sea to sea"). The term was constitutional, used officially for about **100 years**, and remains part of Canadian heritage. **King George V** assigned Canada's national colours (**white and red**) in **1921**; a **1923** Dominion $1 note showed him.

In **1867**, **Sir John Alexander Macdonald** became first Prime Minister. ## Western Resistance, Policing, and the Railway
When Canada acquired Rupert's Land from the Hudson's Bay Company in **1869**, about **12,000 Metis** at Red River had not been consulted. **Louis Riel** led an uprising and seized **Fort Garry**. Ottawa sent troops in **1870**, Riel fled to the U.S., and Manitoba was created. A second uprising in **1885** in present-day Saskatchewan ended with Riel's trial and execution for high treason, deeply opposed in Quebec; many now regard him as a Metis defender and father of Manitoba.

After the first uprising, Macdonald created the **North West Mounted Police (1873)** to help stabilize the West and support treaty talks. The force founded posts that became cities, including **Fort Calgary** and **Fort MacLeod**; headquarters moved to **Regina**. Today the **RCMP** (the Mounties) is Canada's national police force. Notable figures include **Major General Sir Sam Steele**; in Metis resistance, **Gabriel Dumont** was a major military leader.

**British Columbia** joined in **1871** after a railway promise. On **November 7, 1885**, **Donald Smith (Lord Strathcona)** drove the CPR last spike. Financing came from British and American investors, and labour included European and Chinese workers. Chinese communities later faced discriminatory policies including the **Head Tax**; the federal government apologized in **2006**. Early CPR scenes included the Illecillewaet crossing near Glacier, B.C. (**1886**) and Chinese camps near Kamloops (**1886**).

Canadians also fought in the **South African (Boer) War, 1899-1902**: over **7,000** volunteered, over **260** died, and victories at **Paardeberg** and **Lillefontein (1900)** boosted pride. In **1914**, after Germany attacked Belgium and France, Canada formed the **Canadian Expeditionary Force** (later Canadian Corps). More than **600,000** served from a population of **8 million**, mostly volunteers.

At **Vimy Ridge (April 1917)**, the Canadian Corps suffered about **10,000 killed or wounded** and earned a reputation as elite shock troops. **April 9** is observed as **Vimy Day**. From **1914 to 1920**, Ottawa interned over **8,000** former Austro-Hungarian subjects (mostly Ukrainians) in **24** camps. Under **General Sir Arthur Currie**, Canadians helped win the "Hundred Days" in **1918**: **Amiens (August 8, 1918)**, then Arras, Canal du Nord, Cambrai, and Mons. The war ended with the **Armistice (November 11, 1918)**; about **60,000** Canadians were killed and **170,000** wounded. The maple leaf had already been used by Canadian soldiers since the **1850s**.

Women gained voting rights in stages. At Confederation, voting was mostly limited to property-owning adult white men. ## Remembrance, Depression, and the Second World War
Canadians commemorate war sacrifice every **November 11** (Remembrance Day), wearing the red poppy and observing silence at the **11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month**. The text says over **one million** Canadians have served and about **110,000** have died in service. **Lieutenant Colonel John McCrae** wrote **"In Flanders Fields"** in **1915**.

After WWI, the Empire evolved into the **British Commonwealth of Nations**, including Canada, India, Australia, New Zealand, and states in Africa and the Caribbean. Prosperity in the "Roaring Twenties" ended with the **1929** crash and the Great Depression ("Dirty Thirties"). Unemployment hit **27% in 1933**, prairie farmers suffered drought and low grain prices, and pressure grew for social protections such as minimum wage, standard work week, and unemployment insurance. The **Bank of Canada** was created in **1934**. Immigration fell, and refugees were often refused, including Jews fleeing Nazi Germany in **1939**. **Phil Edwards** (born in British Guiana) won Olympic bronzes for Canada in **1928**, **1932**, and **1936**, graduated from **McGill** medicine, served as a captain in WWII, and became a Montreal specialist in tropical disease.

The **Second World War** began in **1939** when **Adolf Hitler** invaded Poland. Over **one million Canadians and Newfoundlanders** served from a population of **11.5 million**; about **44,000** died. Canada suffered heavy losses at **Hong Kong (1941)** and in the Dieppe raid (**1942**). The **RCAF** fought in the Battle of Britain and, through the **British Commonwealth Air Training Plan**, Canada trained over **130,000** Allied aircrew

**, Canada trained over **130,000** Allied aircrewThe **RCN** and Merchant Navy were vital in the Battle of the Atlantic; by war's end, Canada had the world's **third-largest navy**.

In Europe, Canadians fought in **Italy (1943-44)**, then landed at **Juno Beach** on **D-Day (June 6, 1944)** with about **15,000** troops, roughly **one in ten** Allied soldiers that day. Canadian forces helped liberate the **Netherlands (1944-45)** and contributed to German surrender on **May 8, 1945**. In the Pacific, Japan attacked the Aleutians, struck a lighthouse on Vancouver Island, launched fire balloons over B.C. and the Prairies, and abused prisoners captured at Hong Kong. Japan surrendered on **August 14, 1945**. During the war, Japanese Canadians were forcibly relocated and their property sold without compensation despite military and RCMP advice; the federal government apologized and compensated survivors in **1988**.

  "content": {    "en": "## Postwar Prosperity and Social Programs
After the Second World War, Canada saw strong economic growth and rising living standards. Trade barriers from the Depression era were reduced through agreements such as **GATT** (now the **WTO**), and oil discovered in **Alberta (1947)** launched the modern energy sector. By **1951**, most Canadians could afford adequate food, shelter, and clothing; from **1945 to 1970**, Canada had one of the strongest economies in the industrialized world, supported especially by trade with the **United States**.

As prosperity grew, governments expanded social programs. The **Canada Health Act** sets shared principles and a basic national standard of health coverage. Federal unemployment insurance began in **1940** (now called employment insurance), **Old Age Security** began in **1927**, and the **Canada Pension Plan** and **Quebec Pension Plan** began in **1965**. Public education is funded by provinces and territories.

## International Role: Cold War to Security Missions
Like **Australia** and **New Zealand**, Canada developed autonomy gradually while increasing global influence. During the **Cold War**, eastern Europe fell under a Soviet Communist bloc led by **Josef Stalin**, and Canada joined democratic allies in **NATO** and joined the United States in **NORAD**. Canada also joined the **UN**.

In the **Korean War (1950-53)**, Canada participated in the UN mission, with about **500 dead** and **1,000 wounded**. Canada later served in many peace and security missions, including **Egypt**, **Cyprus**, **Haiti**, the former **Yugoslavia**, and **Afghanistan**.

## Canada, Quebec, and Official Languages
Postwar French-Canadian culture flourished, and Quebec underwent major change in the **Quiet Revolution (1960s)**

t major change in the **Quiet Revolution (1960s)**In **1963**, Parliament created the **Royal Commission on Bilingualism and Biculturalism**, leading to the **Official Languages Act (1969)**, which guarantees federal services in **English** and **French** across Canada. Canada helped found **La Francophonie** in **1970**.

The sovereignty movement was defeated in Quebec referendums in **1980** and **1995**. In **1982**, the Constitution was amended without Quebec's agreement, and Quebec's place within Canada remains an active national debate.

## A More Open Society
Over more than **50 years**, Canadian society became more open, with expanded secondary and postsecondary education and more women entering professional careers. Voting rights broadened: many Canadians of Asian descent had once been excluded, and the last major excluded group, **Japanese Canadians**, gained federal voting rights in **1948**. **Aboriginal people** gained the federal vote in **1960**. Today, every citizen aged **18+** can vote.

Canada also received refugees fleeing Communist regimes: about **37,000** from **Hungary (1956)** and over **50,000** Vietnamese after the **Vietnam War (1975)**. Multiculturalism gained strength through **19th- and 20th-century** immigration; by the **1960s**, about one-third of Canadians had origins neither British nor French.

## Arts, Literature, and Film
Canadian cultural life includes major achievements in both English and French. Important literary figures include **Stephen Leacock**, **Louis Hemon**, **Sir Charles G.D. Roberts**, **Pauline Johnson**, **Emile Nelligan**, **Robertson Davies**, **Margaret Laurence**, and **Mordecai Richler**. Noted musicians include **Sir Ernest MacMillan** and **Healey Willan**, while writers such as **Joy Kogawa**, **Michael Ondaatje**, and **Rohinton Mistry** broadened Canadian literature.

In visual art, the **Group of Seven (founded 1920)** became iconic for wilderness painting

nded 1920)** became iconic for wilderness paintingOther major names include **Emily Carr**, Quebec's **Les Automatistes** (notably **Jean-Paul Riopelle**) in the **1950s**, sculptor **Louis-Philippe Hebert**, and Inuit artist **Kenojuak Ashevak**. Canada also built a respected performing-arts tradition with regional theatres and internationally known companies.

Canadian film and media gained global recognition through **Denys Arcand**, **Norman Jewison**, and **Atom Egoyan**, and Canadian television also developed a broad popular audience.

## Sports, Heroes, and National Memory
Canada has produced major amateur and professional athletes in all provinces and territories. **James Naismith** invented basketball in **1891**. In hockey, Canadian teams long dominated internationally, and **Wayne Gretzky** starred with the **Edmonton Oilers (1979-1988)**. At the **1996 Olympic Summer Games**, **Donovan Bailey** became a world-record sprinter and double gold medallist, and **Chantal Petitclerc** became a world champion wheelchair racer and Paralympic gold medallist.

In **1980**, **Terry Fox** of British Columbia, who had lost his right leg to cancer at age **18**, began the **Marathon of Hope** across Canada to raise cancer-research funds; annual events continue his legacy. In **1985**, **Rick Hansen** circled the globe in a wheelchair for spinal-cord research. Other remembered sports figures include **Mark Tewksbury**, **Paul Henderson** (the 1972 **Canada-Soviet Summit Series** winning goal, "the goal heard around the world"), and **Catriona Le May Doan** (gold at the **2002 Olympic Winter Games**).

Canadian football differs from U.S. football. In the **CFL**, teams compete for the **Grey Cup**, donated by **Lord Grey** in **1909**.

## Science, Space, and Inventions
Canada has global influence in science and technology. Thinkers such as **Marshall McLuhan** and **Harold Innis** shaped modern communications theory

Harold Innis** shaped modern communications theorySince **1989**, the **Canadian Space Agency** and Canadian astronauts have supported space missions, often using the Canadian-designed **Canadarm** and **Canadarm2**.

Nobel Prize-winning scientists linked to Canada include **Gerhard Herzberg** (a refugee from Nazi Germany), **John Polanyi**, **Sidney Altman**, **Richard E. Taylor**, **Michael Smith**, and **Bertram Brockhouse**.

Major discoveries and inventions include:
- **Alexander Graham Bell**: developed the telephone concept at his summer home in Canada.
- **Joseph-Armand Bombardier**: invented the snowmobile.
- **Sir Sandford Fleming**: created standard world time zones.
- **Matthew Evans** and **Henry Woodward**: invented an early electric light bulb and sold the patent to **Thomas Edison**.
- **Reginald Fessenden**: helped invent radio and sent the first wireless voice message.
- **Dr. Wilder Penfield**: pioneering brain surgeon at **McGill University (Montreal)**.
- **Dr. John A. Hopps**: invented the first cardiac pacemaker.
- **SPAR Aerospace / National Research Council**: created the Canadarm.
- **Mike Lazaridis** and **Jim Balsillie** of **Research in Motion (RIM)**: developed BlackBerry technology.
- **Sir Frederick Banting** and **Charles Best**: discovered insulin, credited in the text with saving **16 million** lives.

## Cultural and Civic Examples in the Chapter
Named chapter examples also include a **Vietnamese Canadian parade**, an **F-86 Sabre** of the **Royal Canadian Air Force**, **Cirque du Soleil**, **The Jack Pine** by **Tom Thomson**, and Canada's financial centre in Toronto

homson**, and Canada's financial centre in TorontoThe chapter encourages learners to visit museums and national historic sites, including battlefields, archaeological sites, buildings, and sacred spaces, and points to the **Virtual Museum of Canada** and **Parks Canada** for local resources.

"How Canadians Govern Themselves",
  "content": {    "en": "## Core Government Model
Canada’s system combines **three elements**: a **federal state**, a **parliamentary democracy**, and a **constitutional monarchy**. The split of powers was set in **1867** in the **British North America Act**, now the **Constitution Act, 1867**.

## Federal State
Canada has **federal, provincial, territorial, and municipal** governments. The federal level handles national and international files: **defence, foreign policy, interprovincial trade and communications, currency, navigation, criminal law, and citizenship**. Provinces manage **education, health, municipal affairs, natural resources, highways, and most property/civil-rights matters**; **agriculture** and **immigration** are shared.

Each province has an elected **Legislative Assembly**. The **three northern territories** are not provinces, but their legislatures perform many similar functions. Federalism lets provinces adapt policies to local populations and test new ideas.

## Parliamentary Democracy
Canadians elect members to the **House of Commons in Ottawa** and to **provincial and territorial legislatures**. These elected representatives pass laws, approve and monitor spending, and hold governments accountable. Cabinet must keep the **confidence of the House** and resign after a **non-confidence** defeat.

Federal Parliament has **three parts**: the **Sovereign**, the **Senate**, and the **House of Commons**. Provincial legislatures include the **Lieutenant Governor** and the elected assembly. The **Prime Minister** chooses Cabinet and directs government policy and operations.

MPs in the **House of Commons** are traditionally elected about every **four years**. **Senators** are appointed by the **Governor General** on the Prime Minister’s advice and serve until **75**. A bill becomes law only after both chambers pass it and the Governor General gives **royal assent** for the Sovereign.

## Legislative Process (7 Steps)
1

Sovereign.

## Legislative Process (7 Steps)
1**First Reading**: introduction and printing.
2. **Second Reading**: debate on principle.
3. **Committee Stage**: clause-by-clause review.
4. **Report Stage**: further amendments.
5. **Third Reading**: final debate and vote.
6. **Senate**: similar process.
7. **Royal Assent**: after both Houses approve.

In a democracy, participation is a right and duty: citizens aged **18+** should vote in **federal, provincial/territorial, and municipal** elections.

## Constitutional Monarchy and the Crown
Canada’s Head of State is a hereditary **Sovereign (Queen or King)** who reigns under the **Constitution** and the **rule of law**. The Sovereign is part of Parliament and serves a non-partisan role tied to citizenship and allegiance, especially during royal visits. The guide also presents the Crown as a symbol of sovereignty and constitutional freedoms.

As **Head of the Commonwealth**, the Sovereign links Canada to **53** other countries. The chapter names other constitutional monarchies: **Denmark, Norway, Sweden, Australia, New Zealand, the Netherlands, Spain, Thailand, Japan, Jordan, and Morocco**. Canada separates the **Head of State** (Sovereign) from the **Head of Government** (Prime Minister).

Federally, the Sovereign is represented by the **Governor General**, usually appointed for about **five years**. In each of the **10 provinces**, the Sovereign is represented by a **Lieutenant Governor**, also normally appointed for about five years. The chapter identifies **David Johnston** as the **28th Governor General since Confederation**.

## Branches and Regional Roles
Rights and freedoms are protected through interaction among the **Executive, Legislative, and Judicial** branches. In provincial and territorial legislatures, members may be called **MLAs, MNAs, MPPs, or MHAs**. A **Premier** has a role similar to the Prime Minister; in the territories, a **Commissioner** represents the federal government in a mainly ceremonial role.",

federal government in a mainly ceremonial role.",    "fr": "## Modèle de gouvernement
Le système canadien repose sur **trois éléments** : **État fédéral**, **démocratie parlementaire** et **monarchie constitutionnelle**. Le partage des compétences date de **1867**, dans l’**Acte de l’Amérique du Nord britannique**, aujourd’hui la **Loi constitutionnelle de 1867**.

## État fédéral
Le Canada a des gouvernements **fédéral, provinciaux, territoriaux et municipaux**. Le fédéral gère les dossiers nationaux et internationaux : **défense, politique étrangère, commerce et communications interprovinciaux, monnaie, navigation, droit criminel et citoyenneté**. Les provinces administrent **éducation, santé, affaires municipales, ressources naturelles, routes, propriété et droits civils**; l’**agriculture** et l’**immigration** sont partagées.

Chaque province possède une **assemblée législative** élue. Les **trois territoires du Nord** ne sont pas des provinces, mais exercent plusieurs fonctions semblables.

## Démocratie parlementaire
Les citoyens élisent des représentants à la **Chambre des communes** et dans les **législatures provinciales et territoriales**. Ces élus font les lois, contrôlent les dépenses et exigent des comptes. Les ministres doivent conserver la **confiance de la Chambre**; après une motion de **non-confiance**, ils démissionnent.

Au fédéral, le Parlement comprend le **Souverain**, le **Sénat** et la **Chambre des communes**. Dans les provinces, la législature réunit le **lieutenant-gouverneur** et l’assemblée élue. Le **premier ministre** choisit le Cabinet et dirige l’action gouvernementale.

Les députés de la Chambre sont élus tous les **quatre ans**. Les **sénateurs** sont nommés par le **gouverneur général** sur avis du premier ministre et siègent jusqu’à **75 ans**. Un projet de loi devient loi après adoption par les deux chambres et **sanction royale**.

## Monarchie constitutionnelle et Couronne
Le chef d’État du Canada est un **Souverain héréditaire** (Reine ou Roi) qui règne selon la **Constitution** et la **primauté du droit**. Le Souverain fait partie du Parlement et remplit un rôle non partisan lié à la citoyenneté et à l’allégeance, visible lors des visites royales. Le guide présente la Couronne comme symbole de souveraineté, de libertés constitutionnelles et de continuité historique.

Le chapitre souligne l’exemple de service public de la famille royale. Comme **chef du Commonwealth**, le Souverain relie le Canada à **53** autres pays. D’autres monarchies constitutionnelles citées : **Danemark, Norvège, Suède, Australie, Nouvelle-Zélande, Pays-Bas, Espagne, Thaïlande, Japon, Jordanie et Maroc**.

Le Canada distingue le **chef d’État** (Souverain) du **chef du gouvernement** (premier ministre). Au fédéral, le Souverain est représenté par le **gouverneur général**, nommé pour environ **cinq ans**. Dans chacune des **10 provinces**, il est représenté par un **lieutenant-gouverneur**, lui aussi nommé pour environ cinq ans. Le chapitre indique **David Johnston** comme **28e gouverneur général depuis la Confédération**.

## Branches et rôles régionaux
Les droits et libertés sont protégés par l’interaction entre les branches **exécutive, législative et judiciaire**, qui coopèrent mais peuvent être en tension. Dans les législatures provinciales et territoriales, les élus portent selon l’endroit les titres **MLA, MNA, MPP** ou **MHA**
